text:
*[Bug 17549] When building a package, use inRbuildignore() before copying package contents to Tdir*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=17549>

Kurt Hornik (<mailto:Kurt.Hornik@wu.ac.at|Kurt.Hornik@wu.ac.at>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |Kurt.Hornik@wu.ac.at

--- Comment #5 from Kurt Hornik (<mailto:Kurt.Hornik@wu.ac.at|Kurt.Hornik@wu.ac.at>) ---
A few comments.

* This might be a nice task for one of the upcoming R Dev Days (and I
  could imagine joining the task force).

* What should not be too hard is ignoring things at top level (do dir()
  there, drop elts matched from .Rbuildignore, then loop over what is
  left).  

  This would work in my use cases, where I tend to have a sometimes big
  Work subdir which I always have in .Rbuildignore.

  Others may do this differently.  Hard to know on the CRAN end because
  we don't see what gets ignored.  A useful subtask may be extracting
  the .Rbuildignore files for all CRAN package github repos.

* One could perhaps try to put the 

        ## remove exclude files
        allfiles <- dir(".", all.files = TRUE, recursive = TRUE,
                        full.names = TRUE, include.dirs = TRUE)
        ....

  part of the build code into a separate function and optionally run it
  ahead of copying, and then compare performance.  Note that the
  non-windows part of the build code does

            ## This should preserve dates and permissions (subject to
            ## umask, if that is consulted which it seems it usually is not).
            ## Permissions are increased later.
            ## -L is to follow (de-reference) symlinks
            ## --preserve is GNU only: at least macOS, FreeBSD and Solaris
            ##   have non-GNU cp's as it seems do some Linuxen.
            ver <- suppressWarnings(system2("cp", "--version", stdout = TRUE,
                                            stderr = FALSE))
            GNU_cp <- any(grepl("GNU coreutils", ver))
            cp_sw <- if(GNU_cp) "-LR --preserve=timestamps" else "-pLR"

  so I am not sure if file.copy(recursive = TRUE) would be good enough.

  the .Rbuildignore files for all CRAN package github repos.

* One could perhaps try to put the 

        ## remove exclude files
        allfiles <- dir(".", all.files = TRUE, recursive = TRUE,
                        full.names = TRUE, include.dirs = TRUE)
        ....

  part of the build code into a separate function and optionally run it
  ahead of copying, and then compare performance.  Note that the
  non-windows part of the build code does

            ## This should preserve dates and permissions (subject to
            ## umask, if that is consulted which it seems it usually is not).
            ## Permissions are increased later.
            ## -L is to follow (de-reference) symlinks
            ## --preserve is GNU only: at least macOS, FreeBSD and Solaris
            ##   have non-GNU cp's as it seems do some Linuxen.
            ver <- suppressWarnings(system2("cp", "--version", stdout = TRUE,
                                            stderr = FALSE))
            GNU_cp <- any(grepl("GNU coreutils", ver))
            cp_sw <- if(GNU_cp) "-LR --preserve=timestamps" else "-pLR"

text:
*[Bug 19074] parallel::makeCluster(<cluster>, type = "PSOCK") does not give an error*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19074>

Kevin Ushey (<mailto:kevinushey@gmail.com|kevinushey@gmail.com>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |kevinushey@gmail.com

--- Comment #1 from Kevin Ushey (<mailto:kevinushey@gmail.com|kevinushey@gmail.com>) ---
I dug into this. The PSOCK case isn't intentionally accepting a cluster -- it's
just that nothing validates names
in makePSOCKcluster, and the cluster's internal structure happens to satisfy a
code path that was meant for
something else.

Specifically: with cl (a SOCKcluster, i.e. a list of SOCKnode objects) passed
as names:

- is.numeric(names) is FALSE, so the integer branch at snowSOCK.R:164 is
skipped.
- local is FALSE, so the sequential else-branch at snowSOCK.R:246-251 runs:
for (i in seq_along(cl))
    cl[[i]] <- newPSOCKnode(names[[i]], options = options, rank = i)
- Each names[[i]] is a SOCKnode, which is a list. In newPSOCKnode
(snowSOCK.R:87-90):
if (is.list(machine)) {
    options <- addClusterOptions(options, machine)
    machine <- machine$host
}
- That branch was inherited from snow to support per-host option lists like
list(host = "foo", port = 1234, ...). A
 SOCKnode happens to be a list with a $host element ("localhost"), so it slips
through and a fresh worker is
launched on localhost.

Net effect: a brand-new, independent cluster of the same node count is created;
the original cl is left alive. So
makeCluster(cl) silently leaks an entire second cluster.

FORK and MPI fail fast only because their starters coerce eagerly --
makeForkCluster does as.integer(nnodes) at the
 top (forkCluster.R:24) and snow's makeMPIcluster does similarly with
as.double(). makePSOCKcluster has no
equivalent sanity check.

So yes, this should be an error. Two ways to fix it, depending on how broad we
want to be.

Option A: narrow check

Just reject inputs that already inherit from "cluster". Minimal, covers the
reported case uniformly across all
types:

```
Index: src/library/parallel/R/snow.R
===================================================================
--- src/library/parallel/R/snow.R     (revision 89220)
+++ src/library/parallel/R/snow.R     (working copy)
@@ -169,6 +169,8 @@
 makeCluster <-
     function (spec, type = getClusterOption("type"), ...)
 {
+    if (inherits(spec, "cluster"))
+        stop("'spec' is already a cluster object")
     switch(type,
            PSOCK = makePSOCKcluster(names = spec, ...),
            FORK = makeForkCluster(nnodes = spec, ...),

Index: src/library/parallel/R/snowSOCK.R
===================================================================
--- src/library/parallel/R/snowSOCK.R (revision 89220)
+++ src/library/parallel/R/snowSOCK.R (working copy)
@@ -152,6 +152,8 @@

 makePSOCKcluster <- function(names, ...)
 {
+    if (inherits(names, "cluster"))
+        stop("'names' is already a cluster object")
     options <- addClusterOptions(defaultClusterOptions, list(...))
     manual <- getClusterOption("manual", options)
     homogeneous <- getClusterOption("homogeneous", options)
```

Option B: positively validate spec/names

Same fix for the cluster case, but also catches other unexpected values (NULL,
data.frame, environment, ...) with a clear
message. The trick that lets us preserve the (undocumented) PSOCK
list-of-host-specs form while still rejecting
cluster objects is is.null(oldClass(x)) -- a SOCKcluster/SOCKnode has a class
attribute, a hand-written host spec
list (list(host = ..., port = ..., ...)) does not.

```
Index: src/library/parallel/R/snow.R
===================================================================
--- src/library/parallel/R/snow.R     (revision 89220)
+++ src/library/parallel/R/snow.R     (working copy)
@@ -169,6 +169,10 @@

) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |kevinushey@gmail.com

--- Comment #1 from Kevin Ushey (

) ---
I dug into this. The PSOCK case isn't intentionally accepting a cluster -- it's
just that nothing validates names
in makePSOCKcluster, and the cluster's internal structure happens to satisfy a
code path that was meant for
something else.

Specifically: with cl (a SOCKcluster, i.e. a list of SOCKnode objects) passed
as names:

- is.numeric(names) is FALSE, so the integer branch at snowSOCK.R:164 is
skipped.
- local is FALSE, so the sequential else-branch at snowSOCK.R:246-251 runs:
for (i in seq_along(cl))
    cl[[i]] <- newPSOCKnode(names[[i]], options = options, rank = i)
- Each names[[i]] is a SOCKnode, which is a list. In newPSOCKnode
(snowSOCK.R:87-90):
if (is.list(machine)) {
    options <- addClusterOptions(options, machine)
    machine <- machine$host
}
- That branch was inherited from snow to support per-host option lists like
list(host = "foo", port = 1234, ...). A
 SOCKnode happens to be a list with a $host element ("localhost"), so it slips
through and a fresh worker is
launched on localhost.

Net effect: a brand-new, independent cluster of the same node count is created;
the original cl is left alive. So
makeCluster(cl) silently leaks an entire second cluster.

FORK and MPI fail fast only because their starters coerce eagerly --
makeForkCluster does as.integer(nnodes) at the
 top (forkCluster.R:24) and snow's makeMPIcluster does similarly with
as.double(). makePSOCKcluster has no
equivalent sanity check.

So yes, this should be an error. Two ways to fix it, depending on how broad we
want to be.

Option A: narrow check

Just reject inputs that already inherit from "cluster". Minimal, covers the
reported case uniformly across all
types:

Index: src/library/parallel/R/snow.R
===================================================================
--- src/library/parallel/R/snow.R     (revision 89220)
+++ src/library/parallel/R/snow.R     (working copy)
@@ -169,6 +169,8 @@
 makeCluster <-
     function (spec, type = getClusterOption("type"), ...)
 {
+    if (inherits(spec, "cluster"))
+        stop("'spec' is already a cluster object")
     switch(type,
            PSOCK = makePSOCKcluster(names = spec, ...),
            FORK = makeForkCluster(nnodes = spec, ...),

Index: src/library/parallel/R/snowSOCK.R
===================================================================
--- src/library/parallel/R/snowSOCK.R (revision 89220)
+++ src/library/parallel/R/snowSOCK.R (working copy)
@@ -152,6 +152,8 @@

 makePSOCKcluster <- function(names, ...)
 {
+    if (inherits(names, "cluster"))
+        stop("'names' is already a cluster object")
     options <- addClusterOptions(defaultClusterOptions, list(...))
     manual <- getClusterOption("manual", options)
     homogeneous <- getClusterOption("homogeneous", options)

Option B: positively validate spec/names

Same fix for the cluster case, but also catches other unexpected values (NULL,
data.frame, environment, ...) with a clear
message. The trick that lets us preserve the (undocumented) PSOCK
list-of-host-specs form while still rejecting
cluster objects is is.null(oldClass(x)) -- a SOCKcluster/SOCKnode has a class
attribute, a hand-written host spec
list (list(host = ..., port = ..., ...)) does not.

```
Index: src/library/parallel/R/snow.R
===================================================================
--- src/library/parallel/R/snow.R     (revision 89220)
+++ src/library/parallel/R/snow.R     (working copy)
@@ -169,6 +169,10 @@

text:
makeCluster <-
     function (spec, type = getClusterOption("type"), ...)
 {
+    if (!(is.numeric(spec) || is.character(spec) ||
+          (is.list(spec) && is.null(oldClass(spec)))))
+        stop("'spec' must be a numeric count, a character vector ",
+             "of host names, or a list of host specifications")
     switch(type,
            PSOCK = makePSOCKcluster(names = spec, ...),
            FORK = makeForkCluster(nnodes = spec, ...),

Index: src/library/parallel/R/snowSOCK.R
===================================================================
--- src/library/parallel/R/snowSOCK.R (revision 89220)
+++ src/library/parallel/R/snowSOCK.R (working copy)
@@ -152,6 +152,15 @@

 makePSOCKcluster <- function(names, ...)
 {
+    ok <- is.numeric(names) || is.character(names)
+    if (!ok && is.list(names) && is.null(oldClass(names)))
+        ok <- all(vapply(names, function(el)
+                         is.character(el) ||
+                             (is.list(el) && is.null(oldClass(el))),
+                         logical(1)))
+    if (!ok)
+        stop("'names' must be a numeric count, a character vector ",
+             "of host names, or a list of host specifications")
     options <- addClusterOptions(defaultClusterOptions, list(...))
     manual <- getClusterOption("manual", options)
     homogeneous <- getClusterOption("homogeneous", options)
```

I'd lean toward B since the cryptic 'list' object cannot be coerced to type
'integer' from FORK is also a smell,
and a single check at makeCluster cleans that up too. But A is much smaller if
we want to keep the patch minimal.

            FORK = makeForkCluster(nnodes = spec, ...),

===================================================================

+++ src/library/parallel/R/snowSOCK.R (working copy)
@@ -152,6 +152,15 @@

 makePSOCKcluster <- function(names, ...)
 {
+    ok <- is.numeric(names) || is.character(names)
+    if (!ok && is.list(names) && is.null(oldClass(names)))
+        ok <- all(vapply(names, function(el)
+                         is.character(el) ||
+                             (is.list(el) && is.null(oldClass(el))),
+                         logical(1)))
+    if (!ok)
+        stop("'names' must be a numeric count, a character vector ",
+             "of host names, or a list of host specifications")
     options <- addClusterOptions(defaultClusterOptions, list(...))
     manual <- getClusterOption("manual", options)
     homogeneous <- getClusterOption("homogeneous", options)
```

I'd lean toward B since the cryptic 'list' object cannot be coerced to type
'integer' from FORK is also a smell,
and a single check at makeCluster cleans that up too. But A is much smaller if
we want to keep the patch minimal.

text:
*[Bug 18364] Initializing Column Improper Length*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=18364>

Gabriel Becker (<mailto:gabembecker@gmail.com|gabembecker@gmail.com>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |gabembecker@gmail.com
     Ever confirmed|0                           |1
             Status|UNCONFIRMED                 |TRIAGED

--- Comment #3 from Gabriel Becker (<mailto:gabembecker@gmail.com|gabembecker@gmail.com>) ---
This seems consistent with the behavior of a vector outside of a data.frame
context, and thus I'd argue it's "correct", if - as indicated - somewhat
unintuitive/unlikely to be intentional:

```
[1]  1  2  3 NA  7  7
```

In more detail, the way I figure it is that either

1. The length of a new column is initialized to the `NROW(df)`
2. subassignment of the new column occurs with behavior as above
3. the new column is added to `df`

OR

1. The new column is added to `df` as a vector of all `NA`s
2. subassignment into the new column occurs

Both mental models would result in the same ultimate behavior here, which is
what R currently does.

I suggest we close this issue as wontfix.
